The net resistance is R=4.5 Ω+2 Ω+1.5 Ω=8 Ω

Hence, I=VR=4 V8 Ω=0.5 A

The current flowing through the 3 Ω resistor is 

66+3×0.5 =13 A

So, x=1

subject: Physics
q: The stored charge in the capacitor in steady state of the following circuit is ________ $\mu$C.
question: The stored charge in the capacitor in steady state of the following circuit is ________ $\mu$C.
solution:
In steady state, the capacitor acts as an open circuit, meaning no current flows through the branch containing the $100\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor. Consequently, no current flows through the $10\ \Omega$ resistor in series with it on the top right.

The voltage across the capacitor will be equal to the voltage across the $4\ \Omega$ vertical resistor.

Let's simplify the circuit to find this voltage. The $12\ \Omega$ resistor on the left is in parallel with the $12\ \text{V}$ battery, so the voltage across the rest of the circuit remains $12\ \text{V}$.

For the rightmost part of the active circuit, the $4\ \Omega$ top resistor, the $4\ \Omega$ vertical resistor, and the $2\ \Omega$ bottom resistor are in series. Their equivalent resistance is:
$R_{\text{right}} = 4\ \Omega + 4\ \Omega + 2\ \Omega = 10\ \Omega$

This $10\ \Omega$ equivalent resistance is in parallel with the $10\ \Omega$ vertical resistor in the middle. The equivalent resistance of this parallel combination is:
$R_{\text{mid}} = \dfrac{10 \times 10}{10 + 10} = 5\ \Omega$

Now, the total resistance of the main circuit connected to the $12\ \text{V}$ source is the sum of the $5\ \Omega$ top-left resistor, the $5\ \Omega$ equivalent middle resistance, and the $2\ \Omega$ bottom-left resistor:
$R_{\text{total}} = 5\ \Omega + 5\ \Omega + 2\ \Omega = 12\ \Omega$

The total current flowing from the $12\ \text{V}$ source into this main branch is:
$I = \dfrac{12\ \text{V}}{12\ \Omega} = 1\ \text{A}$

The voltage across the parallel combination (the middle section) is:
$V_{\text{mid}} = I \times R_{\text{mid}} = 1\ \text{A} \times 5\ \Omega = 5\ \text{V}$

This $5\ \text{V}$ is applied across the right branch which has a total resistance of $10\ \Omega$. The current through this branch is:
$I_{\text{right}} = \dfrac{5\ \text{V}}{10\ \Omega} = 0.5\ \text{A}$

The voltage across the $4\ \Omega$ vertical resistor is:
$V_{4\Omega} = I_{\text{right}} \times 4\ \Omega = 0.5\ \text{A} \times 4\ \Omega = 2\ \text{V}$

Since the capacitor is in parallel with this $4\ \Omega$ resistor (with no voltage drop across the $10\ \Omega$ resistor in its branch), the voltage across the capacitor is also $2\ \text{V}$.

The stored charge in the capacitor is:
$Q = C \times V = 100\ \mu\text{F} \times 2\ \text{V} = 200\ \mu\text{C}$

Answer: $200$

question: A motor operating on 100 V draws a current of 1 A. If the efficiency of the motor is $91.6 \%$, then the loss of power in units of $\mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}$ is
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & P_{\text {input }}=V i=100 \mathrm{~W} \\ & \eta=\frac{P_{\text {out }}}{P_{\text {input }}}=0.916\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & P_{\text {out }}=91.6 \mathrm{~W} \\ & \text { Loss }=100-91.6=8.4 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A Wheatstone bridge is initially at room temperature and all arms of the bridge have same value of resistances $\left(R_{1}=R_{2}=R_{3}=R_{4}\right)$. When $R_{3}$ resistance is heated to some temperature, its resistance value has gone up by $10 \%$. The potential difference $\left(V_{a}-V_{b}\right)$ (after $R_{3}$ is heated) is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
question: A Wheatstone bridge is initially at room temperature and all arms of the bridge have same value of resistances $\left(R_{1}=R_{2}=R_{3}=R_{4}\right)$. When $R_{3}$ resistance is heated to some temperature, its resistance value has gone up by $10 \%$. The potential difference $\left(V_{a}-V_{b}\right)$ (after $R_{3}$ is heated) is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
solution:
Initially, the Wheatstone bridge is balanced with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$.
Since the bridge is balanced, the potential difference across the bridge is zero: $V_a - V_b = 0$ V.
When $R_3$ is heated and its resistance increases by 10%: $R_3' = 1.1R$
The voltage at node $a$ (between $R_1$ and $R_2$): $V_a = 40 \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 40 \times \frac{R}{R + R} = 40 \times \frac{1}{2} = 20$ V
The voltage at node $b$ (between $R_3'$ and $R_4$): $V_b = 40 \times \frac{R_4}{R_3' + R_4} = 40 \times \frac{R}{1.1R + R} = 40 \times \frac{1}{2.1} \approx 19.05$ V
Therefore: $V_a - V_b = 20 - 19.05 = 0.95$ V

solution:
Colour Letter as an aid to memory Number Multiplier Colour Tolerance
Black B 0 100 Gold 5%
Brown B 1 101 Silver 10%
Red R 2 102

No fourth band

 20%
Orange O 3 103    
Yellow Y 4 104    
Green G 5 105    
Blue B 6 106    
Violet V 7 107    
Grey G 8 108    
White W 9 109    

A set of coloured co-axial rings or bands is printed on the resistor which reveals the following facts :
1. The first band indicates the first significant figure.
2. The second band indicates the second significant figure.
3. The third band indicates the power of ten with which the above two significant figures must be multiplied to get the resistance value in ohms.
4. The fourth band indicates the tolerance or possible variation in percent of the indicated value. If the fourth band is absent, it implies a tolerance of ±20%

 

R=(7500±375)Ω

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following will NOT be observed when a multimeter (operating in resistance measuring mode) probes connected across a component, are just reversed?
question: Which of the following will NOT be observed when a multimeter (operating in resistance measuring mode) probes connected across a component, are just reversed?
options: Multimeter shows an equal deflection in both cases i.e., before and after reversing the probes if the chosen component is resistor.
Multimeter shows NO deflection in both cases i.e. before and after reversing the probes if the chosen component is capacitor
Multimeter shows a deflection, accompanied by a splash of light out of connected component in one direction and NO deflection on reversing the probes if the chosen component is LED
Multimeter shows NO deflection in both cases i.e. before and after reversing the probes is the chsosen component is metal wire.
solution:
Multimeter shows NO deflection in both cases i.e. before and after reversing the probes if the chosen component is capacitor.
Multimeter shows deflection when it connects with capacitor
If we assume that LED has negligiable resistance then multimeter shows no deflection for the forward bias but when it connects in reverse direction, it break down occurs so splash of light out.
The resistance of metal wire may be taken zero, so no deflection in multimeter
No matter, how we connect the resistance across multimeter It shows same deflection.

subject: Physics
q: The current sensitivity of moving coil galvanometer is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. This increase is achieved only changing in the number of turns of coils and area of cross section of the wire while keeping the resistance of galvanometer coil constant. The percentage change in the voltage sensitivity will be:
question: The current sensitivity of moving coil galvanometer is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. This increase is achieved only changing in the number of turns of coils and area of cross section of the wire while keeping the resistance of galvanometer coil constant. The percentage change in the voltage sensitivity will be:
solution:
The current sensitivity is given as

Si=NBAC.

The voltage sensitivity is

Sv=NABCR, where R is the galvanometer resistance.

Since the resistance is constant,
ΔSi=ΔSv

Hence, the percentage change in voltage sensitivity is,

ΔSv=+25%.

subject: Physics
q: In a metre bridge experiment null point is obtained at $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ from one end of the wire when resistance $X$ is balanced against another resistance $Y$. If $X < Y$, then where will be the new position of the null point from the same end, if one decides to balance a resistance of $4 X$ against Y?
question: In a metre bridge experiment null point is obtained at $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ from one end of the wire when resistance $X$ is balanced against another resistance $Y$. If $X < Y$, then where will be the new position of the null point from the same end, if one decides to balance a resistance of $4 X$ against Y?
solution: We have from meter bridge experiment, $\frac{\mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_2}=\frac{\ell_1}{\ell_2}$, where $\ell_2=\left(100-\ell_1\right) \mathrm{cm}$ In the first case, $X / Y=20 / 80$ In the second case $\frac{4 X}{Y}=\frac{\ell}{100-\ell}$ $\ell=50 \mathrm{~cm}$.

q: Figure shows a part of an electric circuit. The potentials at points a, b and c are 30 V, 12 V and 2 V respectively. The current through the 20 Ω resistor will be,
question: Figure shows a part of an electric circuit. The potentials at points a, b and c are 30 V, 12 V and 2 V respectively. The current through the 20 Ω resistor will be,
solution:
Let the potential be x V at the junction.

By using V=IR we get,

30-x=10i1x-12=20i2x-2=30i3

By using Kirchhoff's law,

i1=i2+i3

⇒30-x10+12-x20+2-x30=0⇒90-3x+18-1.5x+2-x30=0

⇒x=20 V

The current through the 20 Ω resistor is 

i2=30-1220 A

⇒i2=820A=0.4 A

Now, Pint=I2intR

and Pfinal=0.8Iint2R

Therefore, % change in power =

Pfinal−PintPint×100=0.64−1×100=−36%

q: A network of four resistances is connected to 9 V battery, as shown in figure. The magnitude of voltage difference between the points A and B is __________ V.
question: A network of four resistances is connected to 9 V battery, as shown in figure. The magnitude of voltage difference between the points A and B is __________ V.
solution:
The resistance in each branch is 6 Ω. They are in parallel. So, net resistance is R'=62=3 Ω.

The total current flowing through the loop,

I=VR'=9 V3 Ω=3 A

Thus, through each branch the current is 1.5 A.

Hence, the voltage difference across the points is

VA-VB=4×1.5-2×1.5=3 V

R2=220×220100=22×22

As resistance are in series current through them will be same.

P1P2=i2R1i2R2=44×4422×22=41

Therefore, among the given options, the option matching is P1=16 W, P2=4 W

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long wire is broken into two unequal parts <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> part of the wire is stretched into another wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math>. Length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math> is twice the length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and the resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math> is twice that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>. Find the ratio of length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>.
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long wire is broken into two unequal parts <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>. The <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> part of the wire is stretched into another wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math>. Length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math> is twice the length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and the resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>W</mi></math> is twice that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>. Find the ratio of length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>.
solution:
​​​​​​​

Resistance of a wire is given by R=ρLA, where, ρ is conductivity, L is length of wire and A is cross-sectional area of wire.

For a single wire, A and ρ are constant.

Thus, for parts X and Y, resistance, RXRY=lXlY.

When wire is stretched to double of its length, then resistance becomes four times, thus, RW=4RX=2RY.

Hence, the ratio is RXRY=12.

So,  lXlY=12

question: For the network shown below, the value of VB-VA is _____ V.
solution:
Combined resistance value in the circuit is, 3 Ω.

i=153=5 A

Therefore,  VB-VA=i×2=10 V

subject: Physics
q: The current flowing through a conductor connected across a source is 2 A and 1.2 A at 0oC and 100oC respectively. The current flowing through the conductor at 50oC will be _____ × 102 mA.
question: The current flowing through a conductor connected across a source is 2 A and 1.2 A at 0oC and 100oC respectively. The current flowing through the conductor at 50oC will be _____ × 102 mA.
solution:
By Ohm's law, V=IR.

Let the current and resistance  at 0° C be i0 & R0 respectively.

Let the current and resistance at 100 °C  be i100 & R100 respectively.

By the given condition,

i0R0=i100R100⇒2R0=1.2R0(1+100α)⇒1+100α=53⇒50α=13

Since the voltage remains constant,

i50R50=i0R0⇒i50=i0R0R50=2×R0R01+50α=21+13=1.5 A⇒i50=15×102 mA

subject: Physics
q: Water boils in an electric kettle in 20 minutes after being switched on. Using the same main supply, the length of the heating element should be _____ to _____ times of its initial length if the water is to be boiled in 15 minutes.
question: Water boils in an electric kettle in 20 minutes after being switched on. Using the same main supply, the length of the heating element should be _____ to _____ times of its initial length if the water is to be boiled in 15 minutes.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{R}}, \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \ell}{\mathrm{A}} \\ & \mathrm{P} \propto \frac{1}{\ell} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{t}_2}{\mathrm{t}_1}=\frac{15}{20}=\frac{\ell_2}{\ell_1} \\ & \ell_2=\frac{3}{4} \ell_1\end{aligned}$

q: In the following circuit, the magnitude of current I1, is ______ A.
question: In the following circuit, the magnitude of current I1, is ______ A.
solution:
The figure above shows the current and voltage at different points in circuit.

Applying Kirchhoff's Junction law at A,

x-(y+5)1+x-22+x-02=0    ...1

Applying Kirchhoff's Junction law at B,

y+5-x1+y-01+y-21=0    ...2

Solving equation (1) and (2), we get

x=3 and y=0

Now, at D junction

I1=i1+i2

⇒I1=y-01+x-02

=0-01+3-02

⇒I1=1.5 A.

After applying shunt

q: For the given circuit, in the steady state, VB-VD= _____ V.
question: For the given circuit, in the steady state, VB-VD= _____ V.
solution:
At steady state current will not pass through capacitor, therefore effective resistance between B and C will be only 1 Ω. Modified circuit will be as shown below.

⇒  iAB=63=2 A  and  iAD=612=0.5 A

Applying Kirchoff's law along path BAD, we get

⇒  VB+2×2-10×0.5=VD

⇒ VB-VD=1 V.

question:

Imagine a parallel plate capacitor is immersed in seawater and is driven by an alternating voltage source V(t)=V0sin(2πft).
